source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 1 PDF
title: Probability space
summary: A probability model is a triple (Omega, F, P): outcomes, a sigma-field of events, and a countably additive probability measure with total mass 1.
proof_idea: Most elementary probability identities are consequences of complement closure, countable union closure, and countable additivity.
exam_use: Use this as the opening language for proofs involving events, limsup/liminf events, and Borel-Cantelli later in Chapter 2.
pitfalls: Do not treat arbitrary subsets as events unless F is the full power set; measurability is part of the model.

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 1 PDF
title: Sigma-field closure rules
summary: A sigma-field is closed under complements and countable unions, hence also under countable intersections and finite set operations.
proof_idea: Use De Morgan's laws to derive intersection closure from complement and union closure.
exam_use: When proving a random object is measurable, show that a convenient generating class of inverse images lies in F.
pitfalls: A countable union is allowed; an arbitrary uncountable union need not be in the sigma-field.

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 1 PDF
title: Continuity of measure
summary: For increasing events A_n, P(union A_n) is the limit of P(A_n). For decreasing events A_n with finite first measure, P(intersection A_n) is the limit of P(A_n).
proof_idea: Write increasing differences as disjoint pieces; for decreasing sets apply the increasing result to complements or differences.
exam_use: Essential for limits of events, almost sure convergence criteria, and first Borel-Cantelli arguments.
pitfalls: For decreasing continuity, the finite-measure condition matters for general measures; it is automatic under probability measures.

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 1 PDF
title: Discrete probability spaces
summary: On a countable sample space, assign nonnegative masses p(omega) summing to 1, and define P(A) as the sum over omega in A.
proof_idea: Countable additivity reduces to rearranging nonnegative series.
exam_use: Useful for checking intuition before proving the analogous statement for general measures.
pitfalls: If the sample space is uncountable, point masses need not determine the whole law.

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 1 PDF
title: Stieltjes measures from distribution-like functions
summary: A nondecreasing right-continuous function with suitable limits defines a unique measure on the Borel line via interval increments.
proof_idea: First define the measure on intervals, verify finite additivity and continuity, then extend to the generated sigma-field.
exam_use: This is the bridge from CDFs to probability measures on R.
pitfalls: Right-continuity is not cosmetic; it pins down the measure on half-infinite intervals and atoms.

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 1 PDF
title: Extension from semialgebras
summary: A measure-like set function on a semialgebra can be extended when it has the right countable additivity/continuity behavior on the generating class.
proof_idea: Pass from semialgebra pieces to finite disjoint unions, then use approximation and countable additivity to extend.
exam_use: Explains why specifying probabilities on intervals or rectangles is enough in many probability models.
pitfalls: Do not assume every finitely additive premeasure extends; the countable condition is the serious part.

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 1 PDF
title: Multidimensional distribution functions
summary: A function on R^d satisfying monotonicity over rectangles, right-continuity, and correct limits defines a probability measure on Borel R^d.
proof_idea: Use rectangle increments and an extension theorem from a semialgebra of rectangles.
exam_use: This underlies joint distributions and later independence statements.
pitfalls: Coordinatewise monotonicity alone is not enough; rectangle probabilities must be nonnegative.

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 1 PDF
title: Distribution function properties
summary: A CDF is nondecreasing, right-continuous, tends to 0 at -infinity, and tends to 1 at +infinity.
proof_idea: Monotonicity follows from event inclusion; right-continuity follows from continuity of probability for decreasing events.
exam_use: Use these properties to test whether a proposed function can be a CDF.
pitfalls: Left-continuity is generally false; jumps represent atoms.

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 1 PDF
title: Every valid CDF determines a law
summary: Any function with the standard CDF properties is the distribution function of a unique probability measure on R.
proof_idea: Construct a Stieltjes measure and check that its half-line values match the function.
exam_use: Lets you prove convergence claims by identifying limiting CDFs, provided the limit is a valid CDF at continuity points.
pitfalls: A pointwise limit of CDFs may fail to be a CDF if total mass escapes to infinity.

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 1 PDF
title: Atoms are jumps of the CDF
summary: For a real random variable, P(X = x) equals the jump F(x) - F(x-) of its distribution function.
proof_idea: Compare events {X <= x} and {X < x}; use monotone convergence from below for F(x-).
exam_use: Useful for mixed discrete-continuous examples and for proving a CDF has at most countably many discontinuities.
pitfalls: A continuous CDF does not imply a density exists; the Cantor distribution is the standard warning.

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 1 PDF
title: Standard normal tail estimate
summary: The standard normal upper tail has exponential decay comparable to phi(x)/x for positive x.
proof_idea: Integrate the normal density and compare using monotonicity or one integration-by-parts style bound.
exam_use: Appears in Brownian motion, LIL-style bounds, and CLT error estimates.
pitfalls: Keep track of whether a bound is one-sided or two-sided; prelim solutions often lose constants harmlessly but not exponents.

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 1 PDF
title: Probability integral transform
summary: If X has a continuous CDF F, then F(X) is uniform on (0,1) under suitable strictness/continuity conditions.
proof_idea: Compute P(F(X) <= u) by translating through quantiles; handle flat parts carefully.
exam_use: Useful for constructing random variables from uniform variables and for distribution transformations.
pitfalls: Continuity alone needs care when F has flat intervals; use generalized inverses for the clean general statement.

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 1 PDF
title: Random variables as measurable maps
summary: A random variable is a measurable map from the sample space to a measurable state space.
proof_idea: Check inverse images of measurable target sets.
exam_use: This is the language needed for sigma(X), conditional expectation, and stochastic processes.
pitfalls: Do not confuse a random variable with its distribution; different variables may have the same law.

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 1 PDF
title: Checking measurability on generators
summary: To prove X is measurable into a generated sigma-field, it is enough to check inverse images of a generating class.
proof_idea: The inverse-image operation preserves complements and countable unions, so the checked class expands to a sigma-field.
exam_use: Use half-lines for real variables and rectangles for vector-valued variables.
pitfalls: The generator must actually generate the target sigma-field; checking too small a class proves too little.

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 1 PDF
title: Composition of measurable maps
summary: If X is measurable and f is measurable, then f(X) is measurable.
proof_idea: Inverse images compose: {f(X) in A} equals {X in f^{-1}(A)}.
exam_use: Use this to justify transformations such as X^2, exp(X), indicators, vectors, sums, limsup, and liminf.
pitfalls: Continuity is sufficient for Borel measurability, but not necessary.

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 1 PDF
title: Arithmetic of random variables
summary: Finite sums, products, maxima, minima, and continuous functions of random variables are random variables.
proof_idea: View (X,Y) as a measurable vector and compose with continuous maps such as addition or multiplication.
exam_use: Often needed before taking expectations of constructed quantities.
pitfalls: Do not use arithmetic closure for extended real expressions without checking undefined forms like infinity minus infinity.

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 1 PDF
title: Limits preserve measurability
summary: Supremum, infimum, limsup, liminf, and pointwise limits of measurable real functions are measurable.
proof_idea: Express events such as {sup X_n <= a} or {limsup X_n < a} using countable unions/intersections of measurable events.
exam_use: Crucial for defining almost sure convergence and stopping-time events.
pitfalls: The countability of the sequence matters; arbitrary suprema over uncountable families can fail measurability.

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 1 PDF
title: Functions measurable with respect to sigma(X)
summary: A random variable Y is sigma(X)-measurable exactly when it can be represented as a measurable function of X.
proof_idea: Approximate Y by simple functions and show each sigma(X)-measurable indicator factors through X.
exam_use: This is a foundation for conditional expectation as a function of observed information.
pitfalls: The representing function is only determined up to the distribution of X, not necessarily pointwise everywhere.

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 1 PDF
title: Simple functions
summary: A simple function takes finitely many values and is the starting point for defining the Lebesgue integral.
proof_idea: Define the integral as the sum of value times measure over level sets, then extend by monotone approximation.
exam_use: When stuck on an integral proof, first prove it for indicators, then simple functions, then limits.
pitfalls: Representations of a simple function are not unique; use canonical level sets to avoid ambiguity.

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 1 PDF
title: Integral of a nonnegative function
summary: The integral of a nonnegative measurable function is the supremum of integrals of simple functions bounded above by it.
proof_idea: Approximate from below by simple functions and use monotone behavior.
exam_use: This definition makes monotone convergence natural rather than surprising.
pitfalls: The value may be infinite; do not subtract two infinite nonnegative integrals.

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 1 PDF
title: Integrable signed functions
summary: A signed measurable function is integrable when the integrals of its positive and negative parts are finite, equivalently the integral of its absolute value is finite.
proof_idea: Define integral as integral of positive part minus integral of negative part.
exam_use: Before using linearity or dominated convergence, check integrability conditions.
pitfalls: Finite positive and negative parts are needed; infinity minus infinity is not an acceptable value.

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 1 PDF
title: Linearity and order properties of the integral
summary: For integrable functions, the integral is linear, monotone, and respects almost-everywhere equality.
proof_idea: Prove first for simple functions, extend to nonnegative functions, then signed integrable functions.
exam_use: Use to split expectations, compare nonnegative variables, and justify replacing variables by a.s. equal versions.
pitfalls: Linearity can fail as a finite statement if one side involves undefined infinities.

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 1 PDF
title: Jensen's inequality for integrals
summary: For a convex function phi and a probability measure, phi(integral f) is at most integral phi(f), assuming the quantities are defined.
proof_idea: Use a supporting line to the convex function at the mean.
exam_use: Prelim workhorse for Lp comparisons, moment generating bounds, and conditional Jensen later.
pitfalls: Convexity direction matters; strict equality often encodes almost sure constancy under strict convexity.

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 1 PDF
title: Holder's inequality
summary: If p and q are conjugate exponents, the integral of |fg| is bounded by the Lp norm of f times the Lq norm of g.
proof_idea: Normalize the functions and apply Young's inequality; handle zero norms separately.
exam_use: Used for moment interpolation, uniform integrability checks, and bounding expectations of products.
pitfalls: Remember the endpoint cases need separate interpretation.

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 1 PDF
title: Minkowski's inequality
summary: The Lp norm satisfies the triangle inequality for p at least 1.
proof_idea: Apply Holder to |f+g|^p split as |f+g|^{p-1}|f| plus the analogous term for g.
exam_use: Use to prove Lp spaces are normed and to control sums of random variables in Lp.
pitfalls: For p below 1 this is not a norm inequality.

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 1 PDF
title: Bounded convergence theorem
summary: On a finite-measure space, uniformly bounded functions converging pointwise have integrals converging to the integral of the limit.
proof_idea: Reduce to dominated convergence with a constant dominating function, or prove using finite measure and small exceptional sets.
exam_use: Useful for probability spaces when random variables are uniformly bounded.
pitfalls: Finite measure is essential; bounded pointwise convergence on an infinite measure space need not preserve integrals.

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 1 PDF
title: Fatou's lemma
summary: For nonnegative functions, the integral of the liminf is at most the liminf of the integrals.
proof_idea: Let g_n be the infimum over the tail; g_n increases to the liminf and monotone convergence applies.
exam_use: Use for lower semicontinuity of expectations and to prove integrability of a limit from bounded expectations.
pitfalls: Fatou gives an inequality, not usually equality.

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 1 PDF
title: Monotone convergence theorem
summary: If nonnegative measurable functions increase pointwise to f, then their integrals increase to the integral of f.
proof_idea: The lower approximation definition of the integral makes the result direct.
exam_use: Use for exchanging sums and expectations of nonnegative random variables.
pitfalls: The monotone direction and nonnegativity are not optional unless you shift or dominate carefully.

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 1 PDF
title: Dominated convergence theorem
summary: If f_n converges pointwise a.e. to f and |f_n| is bounded by an integrable g, then integrals of f_n converge to the integral of f.
proof_idea: Apply Fatou to g + f_n and g - f_n, or to |f_n - f| after a standard argument.
exam_use: The main theorem for passing limits through expectations under a uniform integrable bound.
pitfalls: Pointwise boundedness is not enough; the dominating function must be integrable and independent of n.

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 1 PDF
title: Expected value as Lebesgue integral
summary: Expectation is the integral of a random variable with respect to the probability measure.
proof_idea: All integral theorems transfer directly to expectations.
exam_use: This unifies discrete sums, density integrals, and abstract random variables.
pitfalls: An expectation can be undefined if positive and negative parts are both infinite.

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 1 PDF
title: Linearity and monotonicity of expectation
summary: Expectation is linear for integrable random variables and monotone for ordered random variables.
proof_idea: Apply integral linearity and monotonicity.
exam_use: Use constantly in variance, covariance, martingale, and conditioning calculations.
pitfalls: Linearity does not require independence; product factorization does.

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 1 PDF
title: Markov and Chebyshev inequalities
summary: Markov bounds the probability of a nonnegative variable exceeding a threshold by its expectation over the threshold; Chebyshev applies this to powers or centered moments.
proof_idea: Use the indicator inequality a 1_{X >= a} <= X for Markov, then specialize with nonnegative functions.
exam_use: Core tool for convergence in probability, LLN proofs, and variance bounds.
pitfalls: Check nonnegativity and choose the right moment; a weak moment gives a weak tail bound.

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 1 PDF
title: Limit theorems for expectation
summary: Fatou, monotone convergence, dominated convergence, and bounded convergence all have expectation versions.
proof_idea: Translate each integral theorem to the probability space.
exam_use: A prelim proof often hinges on naming exactly the right convergence theorem and verifying its hypotheses.
pitfalls: Do not cite dominated convergence when you only have bounded expectations; that is a uniform integrability issue, not automatic domination.

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 1 PDF
title: Change of variables formula
summary: If X has law mu and f is measurable, then E[f(X)] equals the integral of f with respect to mu, when the positive or integrable conditions hold.
proof_idea: Prove for indicators, extend to simple functions, nonnegative functions, and signed integrable functions.
exam_use: Lets you compute expectations using the distribution rather than the underlying sample space.
pitfalls: Do not assume a density exists; the law may be discrete, continuous, singular, or mixed.

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 1 PDF
title: Tail integral and nonnegative expectations
summary: For nonnegative random variables, expectations can often be computed or bounded through tail probabilities.
proof_idea: Write X as an integral of indicators {X > t} and apply Fubini/Tonelli.
exam_use: Important for moment bounds, heavy-tail examples, and Borel-Cantelli estimates.
pitfalls: Mind strict versus non-strict inequalities only at atoms; the integral is unaffected in many continuous-parameter formulas.

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 1 PDF
title: Common expectation computations
summary: Chapter 1 reviews expectations for exponential, normal, Bernoulli, Poisson, and geometric distributions.
proof_idea: Use densities or mass functions plus the change-of-variables formula; use series identities for discrete distributions.
exam_use: These examples are quick diagnostic checks for later characteristic-function and limit problems.
pitfalls: Parameterization varies, especially for geometric distributions; state your convention.

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 1 PDF
title: Inclusion-exclusion and Bonferroni
summary: The probability of a finite union can be expanded by alternating sums of intersection probabilities, with Bonferroni inequalities for truncations.
proof_idea: Apply expectation to sums/products of indicator variables.
exam_use: Useful for event-counting, occupancy-style estimates, and bounding union probabilities more sharply than the union bound.
pitfalls: The exact formula is finite; infinite versions require additional limiting arguments.

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 1 PDF
title: Product measures
summary: Given measure spaces, the product measure is the unique measure on the product sigma-field that agrees with products of measures on measurable rectangles.
proof_idea: Define on rectangles, verify premeasure properties, and extend.
exam_use: The measure-theoretic basis for joint distributions and independence.
pitfalls: A set in the product sigma-field can be much more complicated than a rectangle.

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 1 PDF
title: Measurable sections
summary: For a measurable set in a product space, its x-section is measurable in the second space, and section measures are measurable functions of x.
proof_idea: Prove first for rectangles, then close the class under monotone operations.
exam_use: This is the technical engine behind Fubini and Tonelli.
pitfalls: Do not assume section results for nonmeasurable product subsets.

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 1 PDF
title: Fubini and Tonelli theorem
summary: For nonnegative functions, iterated integrals and the product integral agree. For signed functions, the same holds when the absolute integral is finite.
proof_idea: Prove by indicators, simple functions, monotone convergence, then signed decomposition under integrability.
exam_use: Use to exchange sums/integrals/expectations and compute expectations by conditioning-like iterated integration.
pitfalls: Tonelli handles nonnegative functions even with infinite value; Fubini for signed functions needs integrability.

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 1 PDF
title: Product measures as preview of independence
summary: When a joint law factors as a product measure, the coordinates behave independently.
proof_idea: Rectangular probabilities factor, and extension carries this to generated sigma-fields.
exam_use: This prepares for Chapter 2 independence and for computing expectations of products.
pitfalls: Independence is a statement about the joint law, not merely about marginal distributions.

source: Solved Durrett Chapter 1 exercises and exercise viki records
section: 1.1 Exercises: Probability spaces, sigma-fields, generators, and set algebras
title: Countable/co-countable probability measure
summary: To verify the countable/co-countable construction, split disjoint families into the cases where all sets are countable or exactly one set is co-countable.
proof_idea: Prove structural set properties directly from closure rules; use generators/countable bases; build counterexamples by violating finite or countable closure.
exam_use: Exercise 1.1.1 and any probability-space verification on unusual sigma-fields.
pitfalls: Check the hypotheses before reusing the pattern; many Chapter 1 arguments depend on measurability, integrability, monotonicity, or nonnegativity.

source: Solved Durrett Chapter 1 exercises and exercise viki records
section: 1.1 Exercises: Probability spaces, sigma-fields, generators, and set algebras
title: Borel sigma-field from half-open rectangles
summary: Show half-open rectangles are Borel, then express rational open rectangles as countable unions of half-open rectangles; use the countable rational base.
proof_idea: Prove structural set properties directly from closure rules; use generators/countable bases; build counterexamples by violating finite or countable closure.
exam_use: Exercises 1.1.2 and 1.1.3; product-measure generation arguments.
pitfalls: Check the hypotheses before reusing the pattern; many Chapter 1 arguments depend on measurability, integrability, monotonicity, or nonnegativity.

source: Solved Durrett Chapter 1 exercises and exercise viki records
section: 1.1 Exercises: Probability spaces, sigma-fields, generators, and set algebras
title: Increasing union of sigma-fields can fail countable closure
summary: Use finite initial-coordinate sigma-fields on N; their union contains every finite set but misses an infinite non-cofinite set such as the evens.
proof_idea: Prove structural set properties directly from closure rules; use generators/countable bases; build counterexamples by violating finite or countable closure.
exam_use: Exercise 1.1.4; distinguishing algebras from sigma-fields.
pitfalls: Check the hypotheses before reusing the pattern; many Chapter 1 arguments depend on measurability, integrability, monotonicity, or nonnegativity.

source: Solved Durrett Chapter 1 exercises and exercise viki records
section: 1.1 Exercises: Probability spaces, sigma-fields, generators, and set algebras
title: Block construction for asymptotic density counterexamples
summary: Use rapidly growing blocks so the latest block dominates all previous blocks; alternate behavior by blocks to destroy the density of intersections.
proof_idea: Prove structural set properties directly from closure rules; use generators/countable bases; build counterexamples by violating finite or countable closure.
exam_use: Exercise 1.1.5 and density/limit counterexamples.
pitfalls: Check the hypotheses before reusing the pattern; many Chapter 1 arguments depend on measurability, integrability, monotonicity, or nonnegativity.

source: Solved Durrett Chapter 1 exercises and exercise viki records
section: 1.2 Exercises: Distributions, normal tails, transformations, and density calculations
title: Piecewise random variable measurability
summary: For Z = X on A and Y on A^c, write {Z <= x} as (A cap {X <= x}) union (A^c cap {Y <= x}).
proof_idea: Convert distribution questions into CDF/event identities; use jumps for discontinuities; use inverse transformations and normal-tail bounds for explicit calculations.
exam_use: Exercise 1.2.1; patching random variables on measurable events.
pitfalls: Check the hypotheses before reusing the pattern; many Chapter 1 arguments depend on measurability, integrability, monotonicity, or nonnegativity.

source: Solved Durrett Chapter 1 exercises and exercise viki records
section: 1.2 Exercises: Distributions, normal tails, transformations, and density calculations
title: Countability of CDF jumps
summary: Group jumps by size bigger than 1/m inside bounded intervals; each group is finite because total CDF increase is at most one.
proof_idea: Convert distribution questions into CDF/event identities; use jumps for discontinuities; use inverse transformations and normal-tail bounds for explicit calculations.
exam_use: Exercise 1.2.3; monotone-function discontinuity arguments.
pitfalls: Check the hypotheses before reusing the pattern; many Chapter 1 arguments depend on measurability, integrability, monotonicity, or nonnegativity.

source: Solved Durrett Chapter 1 exercises and exercise viki records
section: 1.2 Exercises: Distributions, normal tails, transformations, and density calculations
title: Monotone density change of variables
summary: For Y=g(X) with g increasing, compute F_Y(y)=P(X <= g^{-1}(y)) and differentiate to get f_X(g^{-1}(y))/g'(g^{-1}(y)).
proof_idea: Convert distribution questions into CDF/event identities; use jumps for discontinuities; use inverse transformations and normal-tail bounds for explicit calculations.
exam_use: Exercises 1.2.5 and 1.2.6; lognormal and affine density transforms.
pitfalls: Check the hypotheses before reusing the pattern; many Chapter 1 arguments depend on measurability, integrability, monotonicity, or nonnegativity.

source: Solved Durrett Chapter 1 exercises and exercise viki records
section: 1.2 Exercises: Distributions, normal tails, transformations, and density calculations
title: Density of a square transform
summary: For Y=X^2, use F_Y(y)=P(-sqrt(y)<=X<=sqrt(y)) and differentiate to get [f(sqrt(y))+f(-sqrt(y))]/(2sqrt(y)).
proof_idea: Convert distribution questions into CDF/event identities; use jumps for discontinuities; use inverse transformations and normal-tail bounds for explicit calculations.
exam_use: Exercise 1.2.7; chi-square with one degree of freedom.
pitfalls: Check the hypotheses before reusing the pattern; many Chapter 1 arguments depend on measurability, integrability, monotonicity, or nonnegativity.

source: Solved Durrett Chapter 1 exercises and exercise viki records
section: 1.3 Exercises: Measurability, generated sigma-fields, semicontinuity, and sigma(X) factorization
title: Rational cut proof for sum measurability
summary: Write {X+Y<x} as the countable union over q in Q of {X<q} cap {Y<x-q}.
proof_idea: Check inverse images on generating classes; express measurability through countable rational cuts; prove closure under limits and construct Borel representatives.
exam_use: Exercise 1.3.2; direct measurability of sums.
pitfalls: Check the hypotheses before reusing the pattern; many Chapter 1 arguments depend on measurability, integrability, monotonicity, or nonnegativity.

source: Solved Durrett Chapter 1 exercises and exercise viki records
section: 1.3 Exercises: Measurability, generated sigma-fields, semicontinuity, and sigma(X) factorization
title: Lower semicontinuity via closed sublevel sets
summary: A function is lower semicontinuous iff all sets {f <= a} are closed; sequential closure proves one direction and contradiction via subsequences proves the other.
proof_idea: Check inverse images on generating classes; express measurability through countable rational cuts; prove closure under limits and construct Borel representatives.
exam_use: Exercise 1.3.5; proving semicontinuous functions are measurable.
pitfalls: Check the hypotheses before reusing the pattern; many Chapter 1 arguments depend on measurability, integrability, monotonicity, or nonnegativity.

source: Solved Durrett Chapter 1 exercises and exercise viki records
section: 1.3 Exercises: Measurability, generated sigma-fields, semicontinuity, and sigma(X) factorization
title: Discontinuity set from upper and lower local envelopes
summary: Define local sup and inf over balls, take limits as radius decreases, and identify discontinuities as the set where the limiting envelopes differ.
proof_idea: Check inverse images on generating classes; express measurability through countable rational cuts; prove closure under limits and construct Borel representatives.
exam_use: Exercise 1.3.6; measurability of discontinuity sets.
pitfalls: Check the hypotheses before reusing the pattern; many Chapter 1 arguments depend on measurability, integrability, monotonicity, or nonnegativity.

source: Solved Durrett Chapter 1 exercises and exercise viki records
section: 1.3 Exercises: Measurability, generated sigma-fields, semicontinuity, and sigma(X) factorization
title: Constructive representation of sigma(X)-measurable functions
summary: Quantize Y into dyadic bins, represent each bin as {X in B}, build Borel simple functions f_n, then take a measurable pointwise limit f with Y=f(X).
proof_idea: Check inverse images on generating classes; express measurability through countable rational cuts; prove closure under limits and construct Borel representatives.
exam_use: Exercises 1.3.8 and 1.3.9; conditional-expectation foundations.
pitfalls: Check the hypotheses before reusing the pattern; many Chapter 1 arguments depend on measurability, integrability, monotonicity, or nonnegativity.

source: Solved Durrett Chapter 1 exercises and exercise viki records
section: 1.4 Exercises: Lebesgue integration construction and approximation
title: Zero integral of nonnegative function implies zero a.e.
summary: Use sets {f > 1/n}; each has zero measure because integral dominates (1/n) times its measure, and their union is {f>0}.
proof_idea: Approximate integrable functions by simple, step, and continuous functions; use monotone simple approximations and reduction to step functions for oscillatory integrals.
exam_use: Exercise 1.4.1; null-set proofs.
pitfalls: Check the hypotheses before reusing the pattern; many Chapter 1 arguments depend on measurability, integrability, monotonicity, or nonnegativity.

source: Solved Durrett Chapter 1 exercises and exercise viki records
section: 1.4 Exercises: Lebesgue integration construction and approximation
title: Dyadic lower simple approximations
summary: Approximate f>=0 by 2^{-n} floor(2^n f); the approximants increase to f and their integrals give monotone sums over dyadic level sets.
proof_idea: Approximate integrable functions by simple, step, and continuous functions; use monotone simple approximations and reduction to step functions for oscillatory integrals.
exam_use: Exercise 1.4.2 and simple-function approximation.
pitfalls: Check the hypotheses before reusing the pattern; many Chapter 1 arguments depend on measurability, integrability, monotonicity, or nonnegativity.

source: Solved Durrett Chapter 1 exercises and exercise viki records
section: 1.4 Exercises: Lebesgue integration construction and approximation
title: L1 approximation by continuous functions
summary: Approximate an integrable function by simple functions, approximate measurable sets by finite interval unions, then round step-function corners.
proof_idea: Approximate integrable functions by simple, step, and continuous functions; use monotone simple approximations and reduction to step functions for oscillatory integrals.
exam_use: Exercise 1.4.3; density of nice functions in L1 on R.
pitfalls: Check the hypotheses before reusing the pattern; many Chapter 1 arguments depend on measurability, integrability, monotonicity, or nonnegativity.

source: Solved Durrett Chapter 1 exercises and exercise viki records
section: 1.4 Exercises: Lebesgue integration construction and approximation
title: Riemann-Lebesgue by step-function reduction
summary: Prove oscillatory integral decay for interval indicators explicitly, then approximate any L1 function by a step function.
proof_idea: Approximate integrable functions by simple, step, and continuous functions; use monotone simple approximations and reduction to step functions for oscillatory integrals.
exam_use: Exercise 1.4.4; oscillatory integral estimates.
pitfalls: Check the hypotheses before reusing the pattern; many Chapter 1 arguments depend on measurability, integrability, monotonicity, or nonnegativity.

source: Solved Durrett Chapter 1 exercises and exercise viki records
section: 1.5 Exercises: Lp inequalities, convergence theorems, and absolute continuity of the integral
title: Essential supremum bounds integrals
summary: If |g| <= ||g||_infty a.e., then |fg| <= ||g||_infty |f| and integration gives the L1-Linfty Holder endpoint.
proof_idea: Use Holder/Minkowski, monotone convergence, dominated convergence, and truncation to prove norm, series, and continuity properties.
exam_use: Exercise 1.5.1; endpoint Holder inequalities.
pitfalls: Check the hypotheses before reusing the pattern; many Chapter 1 arguments depend on measurability, integrability, monotonicity, or nonnegativity.

source: Solved Durrett Chapter 1 exercises and exercise viki records
section: 1.5 Exercises: Lp inequalities, convergence theorems, and absolute continuity of the integral
title: Lp norms converge to Linfty on probability spaces
summary: Upper bound by essential supremum; lower bound using sets where |f| exceeds M-epsilon and their positive probability.
proof_idea: Use Holder/Minkowski, monotone convergence, dominated convergence, and truncation to prove norm, series, and continuity properties.
exam_use: Exercise 1.5.2; norm comparisons.
pitfalls: Check the hypotheses before reusing the pattern; many Chapter 1 arguments depend on measurability, integrability, monotonicity, or nonnegativity.

section: 1.5 Exercises: Lp inequalities, convergence theorems, and absolute continuity of the integral
title: Simple functions are dense in Lp
summary: Use pointwise simple approximations bounded by |f|, then dominated convergence on |phi_n-f|^p.
proof_idea: Use Holder/Minkowski, monotone convergence, dominated convergence, and truncation to prove norm, series, and continuity properties.
exam_use: Exercise 1.5.9; Lp approximation.
pitfalls: Check the hypotheses before reusing the pattern; many Chapter 1 arguments depend on measurability, integrability, monotonicity, or nonnegativity.

section: 1.5 Exercises: Lp inequalities, convergence theorems, and absolute continuity of the integral
title: Exchange integral and absolutely summable series
summary: If sum integral |f_n| is finite, then H=sum |f_n| is integrable; dominated convergence applies to partial sums.
proof_idea: Use Holder/Minkowski, monotone convergence, dominated convergence, and truncation to prove norm, series, and continuity properties.
exam_use: Exercise 1.5.10 and 1.7.1 corollary.
pitfalls: Check the hypotheses before reusing the pattern; many Chapter 1 arguments depend on measurability, integrability, monotonicity, or nonnegativity.

section: 1.6 Exercises: Expectation, Jensen/Chebyshev, moment bounds, and expectation limits
title: Equality case in strict Jensen
summary: For strictly convex phi, equality in Jensen forces the random variable to be almost surely constant at its mean.
proof_idea: Translate integral tools into expectation language; use extremal two-point distributions for sharpness, Jensen/Cauchy-Schwarz for inequalities, and MCT/DCT for limits.
exam_use: Exercise 1.6.1; strict convexity equality cases.
pitfalls: Check the hypotheses before reusing the pattern; many Chapter 1 arguments depend on measurability, integrability, monotonicity, or nonnegativity.

source: Solved Durrett Chapter 1 exercises and exercise viki records
section: 1.6 Exercises: Expectation, Jensen/Chebyshev, moment bounds, and expectation limits
title: Two-point distributions as Chebyshev extremizers
summary: To prove sharpness of moment/tail inequalities, place small mass at boundary points and the rest at a value chosen to match moments.
proof_idea: Translate integral tools into expectation language; use extremal two-point distributions for sharpness, Jensen/Cauchy-Schwarz for inequalities, and MCT/DCT for limits.
exam_use: Exercises 1.6.3, 1.6.4, and 1.6.5.
pitfalls: Check the hypotheses before reusing the pattern; many Chapter 1 arguments depend on measurability, integrability, monotonicity, or nonnegativity.

source: Solved Durrett Chapter 1 exercises and exercise viki records
section: 1.6 Exercises: Expectation, Jensen/Chebyshev, moment bounds, and expectation limits
title: Cauchy-Schwarz lower bound for positive probability
summary: For Y>=0, Cauchy-Schwarz applied to Y*1_{Y>0} yields P(Y>0) >= (EY)^2/EY^2.
proof_idea: Translate integral tools into expectation language; use extremal two-point distributions for sharpness, Jensen/Cauchy-Schwarz for inequalities, and MCT/DCT for limits.
exam_use: Exercise 1.6.6; basic Paley-Zygmund-style estimates.
pitfalls: Check the hypotheses before reusing the pattern; many Chapter 1 arguments depend on measurability, integrability, monotonicity, or nonnegativity.

section: 1.6 Exercises: Expectation, Jensen/Chebyshev, moment bounds, and expectation limits
title: Tail truncation for expectation limits
summary: Control small or large tails by splitting the event and using continuity of probability plus elementary bounds on the integrand.
proof_idea: Translate integral tools into expectation language; use extremal two-point distributions for sharpness, Jensen/Cauchy-Schwarz for inequalities, and MCT/DCT for limits.
exam_use: Exercise 1.6.14; limits involving y E(1/X; X>y).

source: Solved Durrett Chapter 1 exercises and exercise viki records
section: 1.6 Exercises: Expectation, Jensen/Chebyshev, moment bounds, and expectation limits
title: Indicator expansion for inclusion-exclusion and Bonferroni
summary: Use 1_union = 1 - product_i(1-1_Ai), expand, and take expectations; partial alternating sums give Bonferroni bounds.
proof_idea: Translate integral tools into expectation language; use extremal two-point distributions for sharpness, Jensen/Cauchy-Schwarz for inequalities, and MCT/DCT for limits.
exam_use: Exercises 1.6.9 and 1.6.10.
pitfalls: Check the hypotheses before reusing the pattern; many Chapter 1 arguments depend on measurability, integrability, monotonicity, or nonnegativity.

section: 1.7 Exercises: Product measures, Fubini/Tonelli, layer-cake formulas, and Stieltjes integration
title: Layer-cake formula via product sets
summary: Represent g(x) as the vertical length of {(x,y):0 <= y < g(x)} and apply Tonelli to integrate sections.
proof_idea: Turn integrals into product-measure regions; use sections and Tonelli to swap order; interpret Stieltjes integrals geometrically.
exam_use: Exercise 1.7.2; tail integrals for nonnegative functions.
pitfalls: Check the hypotheses before reusing the pattern; many Chapter 1 arguments depend on measurability, integrability, monotonicity, or nonnegativity.

source: Solved Durrett Chapter 1 exercises and exercise viki records
section: 1.7 Exercises: Product measures, Fubini/Tonelli, layer-cake formulas, and Stieltjes integration
title: Stieltjes integration by parts with jump correction
summary: Interpret Stieltjes integrals as product-measure masses of order regions; the diagonal overlap contributes the product of jump masses.
proof_idea: Turn integrals into product-measure regions; use sections and Tonelli to swap order; interpret Stieltjes integrals geometrically.
exam_use: Exercise 1.7.3.
pitfalls: Check the hypotheses before reusing the pattern; many Chapter 1 arguments depend on measurability, integrability, monotonicity, or nonnegativity.

section: 1.7 Exercises: Product measures, Fubini/Tonelli, layer-cake formulas, and Stieltjes integration
title: Sliding interval Fubini identity
summary: Write F(x+c)-F(x) as mu((x,x+c]), swap integrals, and observe each fixed t is counted for exactly c units of x.
proof_idea: Turn integrals into product-measure regions; use sections and Tonelli to swap order; interpret Stieltjes integrals geometrically.
exam_use: Exercise 1.7.4.
pitfalls: Check the hypotheses before reusing the pattern; many Chapter 1 arguments depend on measurability, integrability, monotonicity, or nonnegativity.

section: 1.7 Exercises: Product measures, Fubini/Tonelli, layer-cake formulas, and Stieltjes integration
title: Sine integral via Fubini
summary: Represent sin(x)/x as an integral of e^{-xy} sin(x), integrate in x first, and bound the remaining exponentially damped tails.
proof_idea: Turn integrals into product-measure regions; use sections and Tonelli to swap order; interpret Stieltjes integrals geometrically.
exam_use: Exercise 1.7.5.
